corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

context_window.previous: large deep neural networks are powerful but exhibit undesirable behaviors such as memorization and sensitivity to adversarial examples in this work we propose mixup a simple learning principle to alleviate these issues in essence mixup trains a neural network on convex combinations of pairs of examples and their labels by doing so mixup regularizes the neural network to favor simple linear behavior in-between training examples our experiments on the imagenet-2012 cifar-10 cifar-100 google commands and uci datasets show that mixup improves the generalization of state-of-the-art neural network architectures we also find that mixup reduces the memorization of corrupt labels increases the robustness to adversarial examples and stabilizes the training of generative adversarial networks body

top_candidates.sentence: To compare mixup with these alternative possibilities, we run a set of ablation study experiments using the PreAct ResNet-18 architecture on the CIFAR-10 dataset.
In this work, we propose mixup, a simple learning principle to alleviate these issues.
Our experiments on the ImageNet-2012, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Google commands and UCI datasets show that mixup improves the generalization of state-of-the-art neural network architectures.

For instance, the network of used 10 6 parameters to model the 5 • 10 4 images in the CIFAR-10 dataset, the network of used 10 8 parameters to model the 10 6 images in the ImageNet-2012 dataset, and the network of used 2 • 10 10 parameters to model the 10 9 words in the One Billion Word dataset.
On the other hand, neural networks trained with ERM change their predictions drastically when evaluated on examples just outside the training distribution , also known as adversarial examples.
While data augmentation consistently leads to improved generalization , the procedure is dataset-dependent, and thus requires the use of expert knowledge.
Contribution Motivated by these issues, we introduce a simple and data-agnostic data augmentation routine, termed mixup (Section 2).
Despite its simplicity, mixup allows a new state-of-the-art performance in the CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-2012 image classification datasets (Sections 3.1 and 3.2).
To understand the effects of various design choices in mixup, we conduct a thorough set of ablation study experiments (Section 3.8).
The results suggest that mixup performs significantly better than related methods in previous work, and each of the design choices contributes to the final performance.
In all cases, substantial domain knowledge is leveraged to design suitable data transformations leading to improved generalization.
Similarly, in speech recognition, noise injection is a prevalent practice to improve the robustness and accuracy of the trained models .

More related to mixup, propose to augment the rare class in an imbalanced dataset by interpolating the nearest neighbors; show that interpolation and extrapolation the nearest neighbors of the same class in feature space can improve generalization.

More related to mixup, propose to augment the rare class in an imbalanced dataset by interpolating the nearest neighbors; show that interpolation and extrapolation the nearest neighbors of the same class in feature space can improve generalization.
To compare mixup with these alternative possibilities, we run a set of ablation study experiments using the PreAct ResNet-18 architecture on the CIFAR-10 dataset.
Finally, we note that the SMOTE algorithm does not lead to a noticeable gain in performance.
Throughout an extensive evaluation, we have shown that mixup improves the generalization error of state-of-the-art models on ImageNet, CIFAR, speech, and tabular datasets.
However, there are several design choices to make.
